required.title: [Book] Good and Cheap: Eat Well on $4/Day
required.author: Leanne Brown

required.title: [Book] Putting Food By
required.author: Janet Greene, Ruth Hertzberg, and Beatrice Vaughan

suggested.title: [Book] The Omnivore's Dilemma: Young Readers Edition
suggested.author: Michael Pollan

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Pro Home Cooks — meal prep and budget cooking

competence_markers.description: The student can plan, shop for, and prepare a full week of dinners for a family of four within a stated budget
The student considers household dietary needs and preferences when meal planning — not just personal preference
The student can calculate unit pricing and make cost-effective purchasing decisions
The student has successfully completed at least two food preservation projects (freezing, canning, fermenting, or drying)
The student has cooked complete meals from at least three different culinary traditions
The student can conduct a waste audit and implement strategies to reduce food waste
The student can read food labels and distinguish marketing claims from meaningful information
The student has planned and executed a meal for guests — minimum six people — independently

cross_connections.description: Food budgeting is a direct application of household financial management. The student who can manage a grocery budget is practicing the same skills they will use to manage a full household budget.
Preservation connects directly to food production. The student who gardens and preserves is closing the loop from seed to stored food.
The global food supply chain is a modern trade route. Understanding where your food comes from geographically connects to economic geography.
Cooking for others when they are counting on you — on time, to standard, every week — is integrity in daily practice.
Field-to-table food processing connects preservation skills to procurement skills.

content.why_this_matters:
You can cook. You know techniques, you understand nutrition, you plan meals, and you hold a regular cooking responsibility. Now the standard rises again.

This packet is about three things: cooking for others as an act of service, managing food as a real household expense, and beginning to understand how food is preserved beyond the refrigerator. When you cook for your family, you are not just feeding yourself — you are serving people. That changes how you think about it. You plan around their preferences, their dietary needs, their schedules. You budget, because food costs real money. And you begin to learn the ancient skills of preservation — freezing, canning, fermenting — that kept humanity alive before refrigeration existed.

By the end of this packet, you can plan, shop for, and prepare a full week of meals for a family of four within a budget, with nutritional adequacy and minimal waste. That is the benchmark. It is not easy. It is the standard.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Cooking for others** — planning meals around the household's needs, preferences, and dietary requirements
2. **Food budgeting** — understanding the real cost of feeding a household and making intelligent tradeoffs
3. **Food preservation introduction** — freezing, basic canning, fermentation, and drying
4. **Expanded repertoire** — cooking across multiple cuisines and techniques with confidence
5. **Waste reduction** — using ingredients fully, planning for leftovers, understanding the cost of wasted food
6. **Food sourcing awareness** — the difference between industrial and local food production, reading labels, making informed choices
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Cooking for Others

Cooking for yourself is about preference. Cooking for others is about service.

When you prepare a meal for your household, you consider what other people need and enjoy — not just what you want to eat. This means:

- **Knowing your household's dietary needs.** Does anyone have allergies? Medical dietary restrictions? Strong aversions? You cook around these without complaint. The cook who refuses to accommodate a family member's needs is prioritizing their own convenience over the household's wellbeing.
- **Balancing preferences over the week.** Not every meal will be everyone's favorite. But over a week, everyone should see meals they enjoy. This is planning with other people in mind.
- **Timing meals to the household schedule.** Dinner is ready when the family needs to eat, not when you feel like finishing. If dinner needs to be at 6:00, it is at 6:00. Plan your cooking time backward from the serving time.
- **Presentation matters.** Food that tastes good but looks like it was thrown at the plate communicates carelessness. You do not need restaurant plating. You need to serve food that shows you cared. This is a small thing that matters.

### Part 2: Food Budgeting

Food is one of the largest variable expenses in any household. Unlike rent or a mortgage payment, food spending flexes — and it flexes more than most people realize.

**Understanding food costs:**
- A family of four in the United States spends roughly $250-$350 per week on groceries at moderate cost levels (USDA estimates, 2024). This varies enormously by region, dietary choices, and shopping habits.
- The biggest budget drains: convenience foods, eating out, food waste, and impulse purchases. A family that buys pre-cut vegetables, pre-marinated meats, and ready-made sauces spends 30-50% more than one that does the prep work themselves.
- The biggest budget savers: cooking from scratch, buying in bulk, planning meals to use overlapping ingredients, and eating leftovers.

**Practical budgeting skills:**
- **Unit pricing.** The price on the shelf means nothing until you know the price per unit (per ounce, per pound). A larger package is not always cheaper per unit. Check the math.
- **Seasonal buying.** Produce is cheapest in season. Buy tomatoes in August, not February. Buy squash in October. This is both budget-smart and quality-smart.
- **Protein economics.** Chicken thighs cost less than chicken breasts and taste better. Dried beans cost a fraction of canned. A whole chicken is cheaper per pound than any individual cut. Learning to use less expensive protein sources without sacrificing nutrition or taste is a core budget skill.
- **The waste audit.** Track what your household throws away for one week. Most families waste 20-30% of the food they buy. That is 20-30% of the grocery budget going directly into the trash. Meal planning and leftover use are the primary tools for reducing waste.

### Part 3: Food Preservation

Before refrigeration — which became common in American homes only in the 1930s — people preserved food or they starved when the harvest ended. These techniques are thousands of years old. They still work. They are still useful.

**Freezing** — the simplest preservation method. Most foods can be frozen. Key principles:
- Cool food completely before freezing (hot food raises the freezer temperature and degrades other frozen items).
- Remove as much air as possible from containers or bags (air causes freezer burn).
- Label everything with contents and date. If you cannot identify it, you will not eat it.
- Most frozen foods are best used within 3-6 months. They remain safe longer but quality declines.

**Canning** — preserving food in sealed jars using heat to kill bacteria. Two methods:
- **Water bath canning** for high-acid foods: jams, pickles, tomatoes, fruit preserves. Simpler and safer for beginners.
- **Pressure canning** for low-acid foods: vegetables, meats, soups. Requires a pressure canner and strict adherence to tested recipes (botulism is a real risk with improper canning).

**Fermentation** — using beneficial bacteria to preserve and transform food. Sauerkraut, kimchi, yogurt, pickles, sourdough bread. Fermentation predates written history. The process is simple: salt, vegetables, time, and the right conditions. Fermented foods are nutritionally rich and develop complex flavors that cannot be achieved any other way.

**Drying** — removing moisture so bacteria cannot grow. Herbs, jerky, fruit leather, dried beans, sun-dried tomatoes. The simplest drying requires only air and time (herb bundles hung in a dry room). More complex drying uses a food dehydrator or low oven.

### Part 4: Food Sourcing and Quality

Not all food is equal. Understanding what you are buying and where it comes from is part of food literacy.

**Reading labels:** Ingredients are listed in descending order by weight. If sugar is the second ingredient, the product is largely sugar. "Natural" means almost nothing legally. "Organic" has a legal definition (no synthetic pesticides, fertilizers, or GMOs) but does not automatically mean "healthier" or "better." "Free-range" for poultry means the birds had access to outdoors — it does not specify how much access or what conditions. Learn what labels actually mean versus what marketing implies.

**Industrial versus local production:** Most food in American grocery stores is produced at industrial scale — enormous farms, processing facilities, and distribution networks that move food thousands of miles. This system produces abundant, affordable food. It also produces environmental costs (transportation, monoculture farming, water use) and quality tradeoffs (produce bred for shipping durability rather than flavor, animals raised in confinement).

Local food — from farmers' markets, CSAs (community-supported agriculture), or regional producers — typically travels shorter distances, is often fresher, and supports local economies. It also usually costs more. The choice between industrial and local food is a tradeoff, not a moral binary. Understand the tradeoffs and make informed choices rather than ideological ones.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Service Orientation
**Developing well:** The student plans meals with the household in mind, not just personal preference. They accommodate dietary needs without complaint. They time meals to the household schedule. They take pride in serving food that others enjoy.

**Developing slowly:** The student cooks what they want to eat and expects the household to accommodate their choices. They view cooking as their assignment rather than their contribution.

**Diagnostic questions:** Do you express appreciation for their cooking? Cooking as service requires acknowledgment — not effusive praise, but genuine recognition that their work feeds the family. If cooking feels like an unappreciated chore, the service orientation will not develop.

### 2. Budget Management
**Developing well:** The student creates realistic shopping lists, stays within budget, uses unit pricing, plans for leftovers, and can articulate where money was well-spent versus wasted.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot estimate costs before shopping, consistently overspends, or ignores cost entirely in planning.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have they actually done the budget exercise? Understanding food costs requires handling real money and seeing real receipts. If they are planning on paper without checking prices, the learning is abstract.

### 3. Preservation Competence
**Developing well:** The student has successfully completed at least two preservation methods, understands the principles (why salt preserves, why heat kills bacteria, why removing air prevents spoilage), and has used preserved food in meals.

**Developing slowly:** The student completed a preservation project but cannot explain why it works, or avoided preservation activities.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student interested in preservation, or is it being forced? If forced, connect it to something practical: "We have too many tomatoes from the garden — what can we do with them?" Preservation makes more sense when it solves a real problem.

### 4. Week-Long Meal Execution
**Developing well:** The student plans and executes a full week of meals with nutritional balance, reasonable variety, budget compliance, and minimal waste. The household eats well. The kitchen stays clean.

**Developing slowly:** Plans fall apart mid-week. Shopping is incomplete. Some meals are skipped or replaced with takeout. Waste is high.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the full-week challenge realistic for the student's current skill level, or should they work up to it (three days, then five, then seven)? Gradual progression is acceptable. The benchmark is the benchmark, but the path to it can be staged.
